coredata.dc:title:
Boosting photocatalytic NO remove and H2 production by Co(
ii,iii) -modified carbon dots assembly into metal–organic frameworks: The Co(
ii,iii) -modified carbon dots location matters
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Shuang
Zheng, Yuxin
Xia, Wen
Mao, Min
He, Youzhou
Liu, Xingyan
Wang, Jianjian
coredata.dc:description: Inhibiting electron-hole recombination and improving the utilization efficiency of photogenerated carriers perform a central role in photocatalysis. Herein, a “ship-in-a-bottle” strategy and an impregnation method were employed to incorporate highly active Co(II,III)-modified carbon dots (Co-CDs) inside or supported on a representative metal–organic framework (i.e., NH2-MIL-125), denoted as Co-CDs-N-MIL and Co-CDs/N-MIL, respectively, for photocatalytic removal of NO and H2 production. Compared with pure NH2-MIL-125, the photocatalytic activities of both Co-CDs-N-MIL and Co-CDs/N-MIL were significantly enhanced but distinctly different, suggesting that the photocatalytic efficiency closely correlates with the Co-CDs location relative to the NH2-MIL-125. A series of characterizations confirmed that Co-CDs-N-MIL shortens the electron transport distance to some extent, which is conducive to the suppression of electron-hole complexation, and thus has a better efficiency than Co-CDs/N-MIL.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NH2-MIL-125
Carbon dots
Photocatalysis
NO removal
H2 production

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Conversion pathway of photocatalytic NO oxidation on Co-CDs-N-MIL

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: J. Wang thanks to the financial support from State Key Laboratory of Coal Mine Disaster Dynamics and Control (2011DA105287-MS202203), Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (CSTB2022NSCQ-MSX0458), and Joint Fund for Innovation and Development of Chongqing (CSTB2022NSCQ-LZX0030). X. Liu thanks to the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22001026). Y. He thanks to the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22001026), Chongqing Science and Technology Commission (CSTB2022NSCQ-MSX1308), Science and Technology Research Program of Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (KJZD-K202300806). This research used resources of Analytical and Testing Center of Chongqing University. The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for their valuable help in the XPS analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration for the synthesis of Co-CDs-N-MIL, (b) XRD patterns, (c) FT-IR spectra and (d) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of NH2-MIL-125, Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL.
HRTEM of (a) NH2-MIL-125, (b) Co-CDs/N-MIL, (c) Co-CDs-N-MIL and (d) Co-CDs.
(a) Photocatalytic activity of NO removal, (b) the cycling test of Co-CDs-N-MIL.
Photocatalytic hydrogen production performance under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm).
(a) UV–Vis spectra; (b) corresponding Tauc plots, (c) PL spectra; (d) EIS spectra, (e) the transient photocurrents, (f) LSV spectra of NH2-MIL-125, Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL.
Mott-Schottky plots of (a) NH2-MIL-125, (b) Co-CDs/N-MIL and (c) Co-CDs-N-MIL, (d) corresponding band structure diagrams.
The UCPL spectra of Co-CDs under 600, 700 and 800 nm.
XPS spectra of (a) C1s, (b) N1s, (c) O1s for NH2-MIL-125, Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL; (d) Co 2p for Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL.
DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra of NH2-MIL-125, Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL in the dark and under visible light irradiation for (a) ∙OH and (b) ∙O2 − .
In situ DRIFTS spectra of photocatalytic NO oxidation on NH2-MIL-125 and Co-CDs-N-MIL under visible light illumination and an oxygen atmosphere.
The possible photocatalytic mechanism of Co-CDs-N-MIL and Co-CDs/N-MIL under illumination.

head.ce:title:
Boosting photocatalytic NO remove and H2 production by Co(
ii,iii) -modified carbon dots assembly into metal–organic frameworks: The Co(
ii,iii) -modified carbon dots location matters

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed to this work equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inhibiting electron-hole recombination and improving the utilization efficiency of photogenerated carriers perform a central role in photocatalysis. Herein, a “ship-in-a-bottle” strategy and an impregnation method were employed to incorporate highly active Co(II,III)-modified carbon dots (Co-CDs) inside or supported on a representative metal–organic framework (i.e., NH2-MIL-125), denoted as Co-CDs-N-MIL and Co-CDs/N-MIL, respectively, for photocatalytic removal of NO and H2 production. Compared with pure NH2-MIL-125, the photocatalytic activities of both Co-CDs-N-MIL and Co-CDs/N-MIL were significantly enhanced but distinctly different, suggesting that the photocatalytic efficiency closely correlates with the Co-CDs location relative to the NH2-MIL-125. A series of characterizations confirmed that Co-CDs-N-MIL shortens the electron transport distance to some extent, which is conducive to the suppression of electron-hole complexation, and thus has a better efficiency than Co-CDs/N-MIL.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the overexploitation of fossil fuels brings a series of serious crises to the earth, especially environmental contamination and energy shortage [1,2]. Nitrogen oxides (NO x , consisting of ∼ 95 % NO) are a type of environmental pollutant [3]. Typically, NO concentrations in indoor environments are as low as ppb, and even prolonged exposure to such low concentrations can lead to serious respiratory illnesses [4,5]. Selective catalytic reduction (SCR), adsorption, etc. have been reported to be unsuitable for treating ppb concentrations of NO [6,7]. Hydrogen, the most abundant and lightest element on earth, is considered a promising energy carrier for solving the energy crisis due to its sustainable and environmentally friendly advantages [8,9]. Therefore, it is imperative to develop environmentally friendly methods for both removing NO at concentrations of hundreds of ppb and efficiently producing hydrogen in order to cope with the resulting environmental and energy problems.

Since the potential applications of photocatalytic technology in the field of environmental protection and new energy [10,11], NO removal [12–16] and hydrogen production from water cracking [17–21] by photocatalytic technology (especially in the visible spectral region) have been the subject of keen research. The development of efficient photocatalysts to maximize the use of visible light and inhibit electron-hole recombination remains key in this field. To solve these challenges, various catalyst systems have been developed, among which porous catalysts are promising [22–24]. As a porous material, metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) can expose their active sites as much as possible and facilitate substrate contact with the active center by reducing diffusion resistance [25]. Accordingly, during the subsequent photocatalytic reaction, photogenerated electron-hole recombination is largely avoided due to the shortened transport distance for the photogenerated electrons generated by the active center to reach the substrate [26,27].

Introducing electron acceptors into MOFs can enhance photocatalysis. However, the common electron acceptors are precious metal nanoparticles, especially Pt nanoparticles [28,29], which greatly increase the cost of catalysts. Carbon nanodots (CDs) are inexpensive electron acceptors with excellent light absorption properties, up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) properties, and fast electron transfer capabilities [30]. However, low quantum yields (QYs) and insufficient number of surface functional groups are the two main obstacles limiting the application of CDs [31]. The challenge can be tailored by active metal ion chemical doping strategies. For example, Yang [32] et al. reported that CoII dopant can enhance the charge conductivity of CDs. CDs can be immobilized on MOFs by different strategies: in situ formation of CDs in MOFs [30], and pre-synthesis of CDs for subsequent loading onto the outer surface of MOFs [33,34]. Herein, a “ship-in-a-bottle” strategy and an impregnation method were employed to incorporate highly active Co(II,III)-modified carbon dots (Co-CDs) inside or supported on a representative metal–organic framework (i.e., NH2-MIL-125), denoted as Co-CDs-N-MIL and Co-CDs/N-MIL, respectively, for photocatalytic removal of NO and H2 production.

NH2-MIL-125 was chosen as it is highly responsive to visible light, has an intersecting three-dimensional structure and a regular shape [35]. Compared with Co-CDs/N-MIL, Co-CDs-N-MIL greatly shortened the electron transport spacing, thus inhibiting electron-hole recombination; moreover, Co-CDs embedded in NH2-MIL-125 did not aggregate or leach out during the reaction process, which resulted in superior catalytic activity of Co-CDs-N-MIL compared with Co-CDs/N-MIL. The reasons for the excellent photocatalytic performance of Co-CDs-N-MIL were further revealed utilizing XPS, in situ DRIFTS and a series of photoelectrochemical characterizations.

NH2-MIL-125: NH2-MIL-125 was synthesized by a hydrothermal method. 1.43 g (7.9 mmol) of 2-aminoterephthalic acid was dissolved in 20 ml N, N-dimethylformamide（DMF）and 5 ml methanol at room temperature, sonicated for a period of time to dissolve completely and then 1.43 ml (4.85 mmol) titanium isopropoxide was added and sonicated to make the mixed solution well dispersed. The sonicated solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 110 °C for 72 h. After heating, the product was collected by centrifugation and the collected product was washed with DMF and stirred for 3 h to remove unwashed reagents. This step was repeated three times and then the solvent was changed to methanol three times. The final solution was centrifuged and dried in an oven at 80 °C.

Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL: At room temperature, a certain amount of Co-CDs and NH2-MIL-125 was dissolved in 20 ml ethanol solution (ethanol/water: 9/1), and then the mixed solution was sonicated for 10 min. The solution was stirred at room temperature for 6 h and then centrifuged and dried in an oven at 80 °C to obtain Co-CDs/N-MIL.

45.0 mg cobalt gluconate was dissolved in in 20 ml ethanol solution (ethanol/water: 9/1) by sonication at room temperature. 200 mg NH2-MIL-125 was added to the solution and the mixed solution was sonicated for 10 min. The solution was stirred for 6 h at room temperature and then centrifuged and dried in an oven at 80 °C to obtain a solid, which was calcined at 240 °C under nitrogen atmosphere for 2 h to obtain Co-CDs-N-MIL.

The photocatalytic performance of the prepared samples was tested by eliminating NO at the ppb level in a continuous flow reactor. The reactor includes a rectangular glass box with a capacity of 4.5 L and two ordinary LED lamps (12 W) placed vertically above the reactor. Typically, the catalysts (0.1 g) were weighed separately and placed in two sample dishes with a diameter of 12 cm. The test catalysts were dispersed by adding ethanol (about 10 mL) to above sample dishes and then sonicating for 5 min. Prior to testing, catalysts were sonicated and dried utill all the solvent was evaporated, and then cooled to room temperature. Place the processed sample in the middle of the reactor. The initial concentration of NO, which was obtained from a compressed gas cylinder with the concentration of 100 ppm (N2 balance), was diluted to 520 ppb. The flow rates of NO and air were controlled at 15 mL/min and 2.4 L/min, respectively, which could be beneficial to premixing in an air bottle. The lamp was turned on for illumination after the glass container with adsorption–desorption equilibrium. The NO concentration was always detected using a NOx analyzer (Thermo Scientific, 42i-TL). The analyzer also monitors the concentration of NO2, NOx (NOx stands for NO2 + NO). The removal rate (η) of NO was calculated by the following formula:
(1a)
η
%
=
1 - C /
C 0
× 100 %
where C and C0 were the concentrations of NO in the export stream and the entrance stream, respectively.

Photocatalytic H2 production activity was carried out in a 100 mL pyrex reactor sealed with a quartz window. Typically, a certain quantity of photocatalyst (10 mg) and Eosin Y (30 mg) were dispersed in 3 mL of TEOA and 17 mL of deionized water for 10 min. The suspension was first bubbled with nitrogen for 30 min to get rid of residual air in the reactor. Then a 300 W Xe lamp (a 420 nm cut-off filter) was used to initiate the reaction to generate H2, and the lighting power density of the reaction interface was about 550 mW/cm2 via measuring by quantum meters. The gas product was detected and analyzed by a GC gas chromatograph (GC 7920, Beijing) equipped with the thermally conductive detector and a high purity N2 carrier gas.

Normally, the structure of NH2-MIL-125 starts to collapse when the heating temperature exceeds 310 °C [36]. Therefore, we effectively prepared Co-CDs-N-MIL by low-temperature calcination (240 °C) (Fig. 1 a). Co-CDs/N-MIL were obtained by impregnating Co-CDs. The XRD spectra of Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL were consistent with the original NH2-MIL-125 (Fig. 1b). The absence of characteristic peaks belonging to CDs may be due to the small amount of CDs [37]. Moreover, no other characteristic peaks of Co nanoparticles or oxides were observed in the XRD spectra, confirming that the doped Co in Co-CDs was in the ionic state [32]. According to previous reports [38], after pyrolysis and hydrothermal treatment, metal ions in the form of carboxylic acid in metal gluconate could be retained in CDs. Therefore, metal gluconate could be used as an ideal precursor to conveniently synthesize metal ion-doped CDs with special properties and uses.

FTIR spectra showed that the stretching vibration of C-O-C belonging to CDs was at 1048 cm−1 (Fig. 1c and Fig. S1) [33,34,37]. Compared to NH2-MIL-125, the C-O-C of Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL was enhanced. Although XRD and FTIR spectroscopy could only provide some indirect information about Co-CDs, these data confirmed the generation of some heterogeneous species on Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL.

The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and pore size distributions were shown in Fig. 1d and Fig. S2. Table S1 showed that the BET specific surface areas of NH2-MIL-125, Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL were 1396, 1235 and 1074 m2 g−1, respectively. Compared with NH2-MIL-125, the specific surface areas of Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL were significantly reduced, which might be related to the modification of Co-CDs. The pore volumes of NH2-MIL-125, Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL were 0.73, 0.62 and 0.51 cm3 g−1. Apparently, the pore volume of Co-CDs-N-MIL was significantly lower than NH2-MIL-125, further confirming the presence of substances in the pores of Co-CDs-N-MIL, possibly Co-CDs. However, the pore volume of Co-CDs/N-MIL was close to NH2-MIL-125, suggesting that Co-CDs did not enter into the pores of MOFs.

The TEM images of NH2-MIL-125, Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL were shown in Fig. S3, and they showed no obvious morphological differences at low magnification. The HRTEM image as in Fig. 2 a exhibited smooth surface of NH2-MIL-125, whereas Co-CDs/N-MIL had visible nanoparticles on the surface (Fig. 2b) with a measured lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal facets of graphene [32,39,40], confirming that Co-CDs have successfully modified NH2-MIL-125. However, the (100) crystal faces belonging to graphene were not found on the Co-CDs-N-MIL surface (Fig. 2c). Co-CDs-N-MIL was immersed into an aqueous solution of KOH (1 mM), followed by dissolution of the MOFs to produce a suspension of Co-CDs. Co-CDs particles were then obtained by filtration and extraction of diethyl ether. The HRTEM images of the prepared Co-CDs were presented in Fig. 2d, where the inset showed that the average particle size of Co-CDs was 1.37 nm, and the lattice spacing of Co-CDs was 0.21 nm (Fig. S3). The average pore size of NH2-MIL-125 reported in Table S1 was ca. 2.1 nm, which was obviously larger than 1.37 nm. Therefore, Co-CDs could theoretically be formed in situ in the pores of NH2-MIL-125. Figs. S4–S6 showed the HAADF profiles and the corresponding elemental mappings of NH2-MIL-125, Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL, respectively. As can be seen from the Fig. S4, N, O and Ti were uniformly distributed on NH2-MIL-125, and there were obvious Co signals on Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL (Fig. S5 and S6), which proved that the Co element was successfully doped into the CDs.

To investigate the photocatalytic performance of the prepared catalysts, the catalysts were applied for photocatalytic NO removal. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the removal of NO (520 ppm) was only 0.7 % when there was no catalyst, indicating that NO did not remove itself under light conditions. When pure NH2-MIL-125 was used as the catalyst, the NO removal rate was 26.8 %. The NO removal rate could be enhanced to 46.3 % when NH2-MIL-125 was loaded with Co-CDs, indicating that Co-CDs could promote the photocatalytic activity of NH2-MIL-125. Then, when Co-CDs-N-MIL was used to remove NO, this removal rate could be greatly enhanced to 67.6 %, indicating that the catalysts formed through the two synthesis methods exhibited different photocatalytic activities. Fig. 3b examined the cyclic stability of Co-CDs-N-MIL where the catalyst was recovered by ethanol washing. After the fifth recirculation, there was an insignificant decrease in NO removal, indicating that this catalyst could be recirculated. The XRD and TEM of the recovered catalysts were shown in Fig. S8, and the results indicated that the structure and morphology of the recovered catalysts did not undergo significant changes. In addition, we probed the yield of NO2 (Fig. S9), a pollutant produced by the partial oxidation of NO induced by O2 molecules. It could be seen that when Co-CDs-N-MIL was used to remove NO, almost no NO2 was produced, thus avoiding secondary pollution.

Photocatalytic H2 production of the as-prepared catalysts was evaluated in the presence of EY (photosensitizer) and triethanolamine (a sacrificial agent) (Fig. 4 ). Very little H2 evolution was detected over Co-CDs, NH2-MIL-125 and Co-CDs/N-MIL. However, the hydrogen-producing activity of Co-CDs-N-MIL was much higher than that of NH2-MIL-125 and Co-CDs/N-MIL. Specifically, the H2 production rates of Co-CDs, NH2-MIL-125 and Co-CDs/N-MIL reached 85.4, 213.0 and 2227.5 μmol g-1 h−1, respectively, whereas the hydrogen production rate of Co-CDs-N-MIL reached 7313.2 μmol g-1 h−1, which was 34.3-fold higher than that of NH2-MIL-125. We found that no hydrogen was produced when only EY or Co-CDs-N-MIL was present, suggesting that there was a rapid electron migration between EY and Co-CDs-N-MIL, which facilitated the production of H2. When physically mixing EY, Co-CDs and NH2-MIL-125, the hydrogen production rate was 838.1 g-1h−1, much less than that of Co-CDs-N-MIL, suggesting that Co-CDs and NH2-MIL-125 did not contribute to the photocatalytic activity purely through physical interactions, but rather due to intermolecular charge transfer.

The light-absorbing ability and the photogenerated electron-hole separation ability of the catalyst were the two main factors determining the photocatalytic activity. The UV–visible spectrum of NH2-MIL-125 was shown in Fig. 5
a, where absorption bands in the range of 400–800 nm were observed. Co-CDs/N-MIL showed similar absorption profiles to NH2-MIL-125, with an increase in its absorption in the visible region due to the loading of Co-CDs. The light-absorbing properties of Co-CDs-N-MIL were substantially enhanced, and the increase in absorption may arise from the n-π* and π-π* transitions of carbonized substances in cobalt gluconate [41,42]. Tauc plots of NH2-MIL-125, Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL were shown in Fig. 5b. The results indicated that the band gaps of the three samples were 2.58, 2.51, and 2.33 eV, respectively, which suggested that the formation of Co-CDs on NH2-MIL-125 by “ship-in-a-bottle” method significantly affected the energy-band structure.

To investigate the charge separation properties of the catalysts, the samples were characterized using PL spectroscopy (Fig. 5c). At an excitation wavelength of 327 nm, NH2-MIL-125 showed a broad and strong peak in the wavelength range of 450–550 nm, which was the fluorescence emission caused by photogenerated electron-hole recombination. Usually, the lower PL intensity implied the more difficult photogenerated electron-hole recombination. After loading Co-CDs on NH2-MIL-125, the fluorescence emitted from Co-CDs/N-MIL was quenched and the emission intensity was reduced, indicating that Co-CDs could act as effective electron reservoirs to hinder the recombination of photoinduced carriers in NH2-MIL-125 under light. The PL intensity of Co-CDs-N-MIL showed a further decrease, indicating that electrons could be transferred from NH2-MIL-125 to Co-CDs more rapidly in Co-CDs-N-MIL, thus retaining holes in the valence band of NH2-MIL-125 and avoiding photogenerated electron-hole recombination. In addition, the charge transfer resistance (Rct) of catalysts was detected by EIS spectroscopy.

Generally, the diameter of the semicircle in the EIS spectra is equal to the Rct of the catalyst. As shown in Fig. 5d, the Rct values were in the order of NH2-MIL-125 > Co-CDs/N-MIL>Co-CDs-N-MIL. Co-CDs-N-MIL had the smallest Rct, which was favorable for the separation of photogenerated electrons-holes. As shown in Fig. 5e, Co-CDs could enhance the transient photocurrent of NH2-MIL-125, which implied that the photogenerated electron-hole separation in NH2-MIL-125 was improved. Co-CDs-N-MIL possessed the strongest transient photocurrent signal, which was well matched with its photocatalytic activity, demonstrating that the separation of photogenerated charges was one of the determining factors for photocatalytic performance. The LSV analysis results were shown in Fig. 5f. It was observed that the current density of Co-CDs-N-MIL was the largest, indicating a strong response of Co-CDs-N-MIL to the photovoltage.

Fig. 6 was the corresponding band structure diagrams. The redox potentials of EY and EY* versus (vs.) normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) is 1.10 and −1.05 V [43,44]. The electronic band structures were investigated by Mott-Schottky plots. From Fig. 6a–c, it was found that NH2-MIL-125, Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL showed positive slopes in the Mott-Schottky plots at a frequency of 1000 Hz, indicating that they were n-type semiconductors. The flat-band positions (Efb) of NH2-MIL-125, Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL were approximately −0.89, −1.03 and −1.08 V vs. SCE, respectively, when using the saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as a reference. For n-type semiconductors, Efb was generally positive by 0.2 V over the bottom of the conduction band (ECB). The ECB of NH2-MIL-125, Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL were calculated to be approximately −1.09, −1.23 and −1.28 V vs. SCE, respectively. And the ECB of NH2-MIL-125, Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL compared to normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) were estimated to be −0.85, −0.99 and −1.04 V vs. NHE (ENHE=ESCE+0.241 V) [41]. Combined with the band gap diagram (Fig. 6d), the valence band (EVB) positions of NH2-MIL-125, Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL could be calculated as 1.73, 1.52 and 1.29 V vs. NHE, respectively.

UCPL was a unique property of CDs. As shown in Fig. 7 , to illustrate the role of Co-CDs in driving the visible light response of NH2-MIL-125, we obtained UCPL data of Co-CDs. At excitation wavelengths of 600, 700, and 800 nm, Co-CDs emit shorter wavelengths of light, and most of the emitted light was concentrated in the visible region. Therefore, the light emitted by Co-CDs could excite NH2-MIL-125 under visible light. The unique UCPL function of Co-CDs improved the utilization of light by NH2-MIL-125, thus stimulating its production of more photogenerated carriers.

The electronic structure of the catalyst was characterized by XPS. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the C 1 s spectrum of NH2-MIL-125 existed three significant peaks located at 284.8, 286.2, and 288.8 eV, which corresponded to C=C, C-N and C=O, respectively [38,39]. Fig. 8b showed the N 1 s spectra, and the peaks located at 399.3, 400.4, and 402.0 eV were attributed to C=N, C-N, and N–H, respectively. Tables S2 and S3 revealed that Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL had higher C-N content than NH2-MIL-125. It was probably due to the formation of C-N bonds between C atoms in Co-CDs and –NH2 of NH2-MIL-125, revealing that Co-CDs successfully modified NH2-MIL-125. Compared to NH2-MIL-125, the C-N bonds of Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL were shifted toward higher binding energies, possibly due to the transfer of electrons from NH2-MIL-125 to Co-CDs.

The O 1 s as in Fig. 8c, in which the peaks located at 530.2 and 531.8 eV were attributed to Ti-O and O–H, respectively. The obvious extra C-O bond in Co-CDs-N-MIL may be attributed to the formation of Ti-O-C bond between the carboxyl/hydroxyl groups of Co-CDs and Ti-OH of NH2-MIL-125, which further confirmed that Co-CDs were more easily grown in situ in the pores of NH2-MIL-125.

CoII and CoIII complexes had been reported to act as reactive centers (especially proton reduction centers) [36,45], capable of reducing protons to H2 once photoelectrons were received. Therefore, it is necessary to explore the valence state of Co. As shown in Fig. 8d, peaks at 786.2 and 801.6 eV were attributed to CoII, while peaks at 781.6 and 797.3 eV belonged to CoIII [46,47]. The Co(II, III) content in Co-CDs/N-MIL and Co-CDs-N-MIL was 83.7 % and 92.3 %, respectively (Table S4). Apparently, more Co(II, III) promoted the photocatalytic activity of Co-CDs-N-MIL.

The ESR technique (DMPO- as trapping agent) was utilized to detect the reactive groups produced under visible light irradiation (Fig. 9 ). In the absence of light irradiation, neither DMPO-O2 − nor DMPO-OH spin adducts were detected. The typical ESR signal characteristic peaks of DMPO-O2 − and DMPO-OH adducts were observed for 5 min of visible light irradiation, and the O2 − and OH radical signals were enhanced as the light irradiation time was extended to 10 min, confirming that O2– and OH radicals were produced during the photocatalytic reaction. The O2 − and OH radical signals followed the following pattern: Co-CDs-NM>Co-CDs/NM>NH2-MIL-125, suggesting that Co-CDs can induce more radicals to be produced and the location of Co-CDs was crucial, which was responsible for the enhanced photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conversion pathway of photocatalytic NO oxidation on Co-CDs-N-MIL
ce:section.ce:para: In order to reveal the mechanism of photocatalytic removal of NO, we used in situ DRIFTS to dynamically examine the reaction intermediates and final products of NH2-MIL-125 and Co-CDs-N-MIL in the oxidation of NO (Fig. 10 ). When the light was not turned on, as shown in Fig. 10a, the bands of 1338 cm−1 (N2O3) [48], 1318 cm−1 (cis-N2O2 2-) [48], 1282–1127 cm−1 (NO+) [48], 1102 cm−1 (NO−) [49], 1063 cm−1 (NO3 −) [50], and 1016 cm-1 (NO3 −) [50] were noticed. In Fig. 10b, the bands of 1338 cm−1 (N2O3), 1321 cm−1 (cis-N2O2 2-) [48], 1318 cm−1 (cis-N2O2 2-), 1300–1126 cm−1 (NO+) [48], 1102 cm−1 (NO−) and 1084–1010 cm−1 (NO3 −) [48,50] were noticed. The conversion pathways of NO could be deduced by the equations: NO+h+ → NO+, 2NO+2e- → 2NO− → cis-N2O2 2-, 2NO+ + 1/2O2 + 2e-→ N2O3 and NO+O2 + e- → NO3 −.

Subsequently turning the light on, we found that for NH2-MIL-125 (Fig. 10c), cis-N2O2 2- located at 1318 cm−1 disappeared with increasing light time, and NO3 − located at 1063 cm−1 and 1016 cm−1 shifted to 1054 cm−1 and 1021 cm−1, respectively, with a decrease in the peak intensity, which predicted a decrease in NO3 −, indicating that NH2-MIL-125 was not positively corresponding to light. For Co-CDs-N-MIL (Fig. 10d), N2O3 located at 1338 cm−1 completely disappeared, and new NO3 – located at 1046 cm−1 and 1014 cm−1 was added, and the peak intensity was enhanced with the prolongation of light, indicating that Co-CDs-N-MIL oxidized the nitrogen oxides to NO3 − under the effect of light. This could be attributed to the ROS (O2 −, OH) participating in photocatalytic oxidation: NO/NO+/N2O3 + ROS→NO2 −/NO3 − and 2NO− →cis-N2O2 2- + ROS→NO2 −.

Under short-wavelength visible light irradiation, NH2-MIL-125 were excited and produced photogenerated electron-hole pairs (formulas (1)). Co-CDs prepared in situ in NH2-MIL-125 generated more CoII and CoIII, effectively separating photogenerated electron-hole pairs. In addition, Co-CDs could serve as localized electron acceptors to harvest electrons, which was the main function of these embedded Co-CDs to enhance the photocatalytic activity of NH2-MIL-125. Through the up-conversion effect, Co-CDs absorb long-wavelength light (e.g., NIR-visible) and emit short-wavelength light (e.g., UV-visible), which continues to be absorbed and utilized by NH2-MIL-125. Therefore, Co-CDs could improve the light absorption of NH2-MIL-125 in the long-wavelength visible light region.

Combining the above analysis and results, we proposed a schematic of the mechanism of photocatalytic removal of NO and hydrogen production on Co-CDs-N-MIL (Scheme 1 ). The schematic showed that the O2 could be reduced to O2 − and OH by photogenerated electrons on Co-CDs-N-MIL (formulas (3)–(5)). The generation of O2 − and OH was verified in the previous ESR discussion section. The O2 − and OH were key reactive radicals for the conversion of NO to NO2 − and NO3 − (formulas (6)–(8)). In the photocatalytic hydrogen production system, with the presence of visible light, EY molecules injected photogenerated electrons into Co-CDs-N-MIL (M−S plots in Fig. 6 display the energy band positions of EY and MOFs with discussion), and then H+ could capture the photogenerated electrons to generate hydrogen (formulas (9)). These processes be described by the following equations:
Co-CDs (up-conversion): λNIR-visible → λUV–visible
(1) NH2-MIL-125 → e- + h+
(2) H2O→OH− + H+
(3) e- + O2 →∙O2 −
(4) ∙O2 − + 2H+ + e- → H2O2
(5) H2O2 + e- → ∙OH+OH−
(6) NO + OH→NO+ + OH−
(7) NO+ + 2OH+2OH− → NO3 − + 2H2O
(8)
O2 − + NO+ + e- → NO3 −
(9) 2e- + 2H+ → H2

In this work, we designed and fabricated a photocatalytic material of MOFs embedded with Co-CDs for photocatalytic removal of NO and hydrogen production via a “ship-in-a-bottle” strategy. At a mild pyrolysis temperature, cobalt gluconate could be converted into ultra-small Co-CDs embedded in NH2-MIL-125, and this mild pyrolysis strategy could preserve the structure and active sites of the MOFs for efficient mass transfer. Meanwhile, the synergistic interaction between NH2-MIL-125 and Co-CDs ensures that Co-CDs-N-MIL has a wide range of solar light absorption, and Co-CDs prepared in situ on NH2-MIL-125 could form more CoII and CoIII, which promoted rapid charge separation. Therefore, Co-CDs-N-MIL exhibited high photocatalytic activity for photocatalytic NO removal and hydrogen production.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

This research used resources of Analytical and Testing Center of Chongqing University. The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for their valuable help in the XPS analysis.
